medical.title: A hidden DNA genome protector may explain why health and aging differ between men and women
medical.summary: How diseases develop and how the body ages can differ between females and males, but the biological reasons for these differences are not fully understood. Researchers are studying the role of sex chromosomes to better understand what may be driving these differences.

medical.title: Pregnant women may reduce key health risk through less sitting, more light exercise
medical.summary: Women who engage in light physical activity and lessen their sedentary time may significantly reduce the risk of key health problems during pregnancy, according to a new University of Iowa-led study. The paper, "Optimal 24-hour movement behaviour compositions across trimesters and risk of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy: the Pregnancy 24/7 cohort study," is published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine.

medical.title: Transparency is vital for AI usage in health care and patient–provider relationships, researchers find
medical.summary: Among the top artificial intelligence companies, the current race is ultimately to build better, faster and more accurate algorithms. Artificial intelligence (AI) has become commonplace in every sector, including health care, and an increasing number of primary care physicians are turning to AI for everything from diagnoses to patient note transcriptions.

medical.title: Why the liquid you take with your medication matters
medical.summary: Some alkaline mineral and medicinal waters may weaken the enteric coating of medications within just a few minutes, potentially reducing their effectiveness, according to a new study by Semmelweis University. The study, published in Pharmaceutics, found that if the active ingredient is released too early in the stomach rather than in the intestinal tract, it may reduce—and in extreme cases, even eliminate—the effectiveness of certain anti-reflux, gastroprotective, psychiatric or anti-inflammatory pain-relief medications.

medical.title: From skipping meals to selling assets: COVID-19 and coping strategies of vulnerable Indian households
medical.summary: The COVID-19 pandemic pushed some households in India into difficult and often unsustainable coping strategies, forcing tradeoffs between immediate survival and long-term stability, according to new research by Lancaster University and the Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur (IITK).

medical.title: Girls are biggest consumers of anxiolytic and sedative drugs among adolescents in most EU countries
medical.summary: Xabi Martinez-Mendia, a researcher at the EHU-University of the Basque Country, has analyzed how adolescent psychotropic drug consumption is linked to gender inequality and economic factors in 32 European countries. Published in the European Journal of Public Health, the results indicate that overall consumption of psychotropic drugs is on the rise in countries with greater gender and economic parity, whereas in countries with lower parity, these drugs are consumed proportionally more by girls, and the gender gap is wider.

medical.title: Drug reduces the risk of developing rheumatoid arthritis, clinical trial finds
medical.summary: A clinical trial led by researchers from Hospital Clínic de Barcelona, the August Pi i Sunyer Biomedical Research Institute (IDIBAPS) and the Institute of Advanced Chemistry of Catalonia (IQAC-CSIC) compared the effectiveness of abatacept and hydroxychloroquine in preventing the development of rheumatoid arthritis in patients with palindromic rheumatism, an autoimmune disease that progresses to arthritis in approximately half of all patients.

medical.title: Online grocery shopping could bring more fresh produce to New York's SNAP families
medical.summary: The high cost of fresh fruits and vegetables and the need to restock them frequently can be barriers to healthy eating for low-income families. New research shows that online grocery shopping, combined with incentive programs, can help.

medical.title: How modern rheumatoid arthritis treatments can protect bone health
medical.summary: A new review published in Calcified Tissue International highlights major advances in understanding and preventing bone loss in people with rheumatoid arthritis (RA), showing that modern antirheumatic therapies can significantly reduce local and generalized bone loss in RA.

medical.title: Surgical menopause causes more severe genitourinary syndrome of menopause phenotype
medical.summary: Menopause is associated with an array of sexual and urinary symptoms, although not all women experience the same symptoms or severity. A new study suggests that many of these signs and symptoms are worse for women who undergo surgical menopause instead of natural menopause. Results of the study are published today in Menopause.

medical.title: Decades-old puzzle solved as scientists uncover cause of inflammatory bowel disease
medical.summary: Researchers at the Nuffield Department of Medicine, University of Oxford, together with Newcastle University's Translational and Clinical Research Institute and the Department of Immunology at Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, have identified an important driver of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). This discovery reshapes understanding of IBD and opens the way to targeted approaches to diagnosis and treatment in a subset of patients. The findings suggest that inflammatory bowel disease is not a single condition, but a group of biologically distinct diseases driven by different underlying mechanisms.

medical.title: New protein targets reveal 13 drug repurposing leads for progressive multiple sclerosis
medical.summary: A new study identified protein targets and drug candidates, including opportunities to repurpose existing drugs. Six key proteins were highlighted as potentially providing new insights and supporting future treatment development for progressive multiple sclerosis (MS).

medical.title: Difficulty conceiving tied to small neurodevelopmental differences in children
medical.summary: Difficulty conceiving a pregnancy may be associated with small differences in children's learning and behavior, according to a new study, published in the journal JAMA Network Open, titled "Associations of subfecundity and infertility treatment with child neurodevelopment in ECHO."

medical.title: Ten-week therapy empowers parents to solve severe selective eating in children with autism
medical.summary: Picky eating is a challenge most parents are familiar with, but for parents of autistic children, severe selective eating can lead to nutritional deficiencies and place tremendous stress on the family. However, a new study from Constructor University Ph.D. candidate Sofya Bajaa has demonstrated a transformative new approach to treating severe selective eating in children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Bajaa's newly developed Schmetterling Nutritional Behavior Intervention (NBI) program achieved dramatic improvements in dietary variety and nutritional intake over just 10 weeks.

science.title: Degradable sensors reveal hidden soil secrets after microbes nibble on them
science.summary: New degradable sensors could reveal insights into the secret—yet essential—microscopic life in soil. The sensors, which are being developed by soil scientists at Lancaster University and researchers at the University of Colorado Boulder and the University of Manchester, track biological activity in soil by using a degradable substrate that is nibbled on by microbes.

science.title: Lab-created 'moon' rock could help scientists interpret lunar data and explore how water might form on the moon
science.summary: The moon may look unchanged from afar, but its surface is constantly reshaped by microscopic impacts and a steady stream of particles from the sun, a process known as space weathering. Now, Georgia Tech researchers have recreated one of those weathering sources, solar wind, in the lab—offering new insight into how the lunar surface evolves. Their work is published in The Planetary Science Journal.

science.title: Low-copper paints matched high-copper rivals, while silicone performed best against fouling
science.summary: When comparing different types of antifouling paints against fouling on leisure boats, the results were the opposite of what many would expect. Of the paints tested, the biocide-free silicone paint worked best, and the paint marketed as environmentally friendly turned out to be extremely toxic. The study, led by Chalmers University of Technology in Sweden, was conducted in Swedish, Danish and French coastal waters and also showed that coatings with a low copper content can be just as effective as those containing more copper.

science.title: Novel catalyst design boosts solar-driven ammonia production under mild conditions
science.summary: Sunlight, water, air and metal-organic catalysts—that could be all it takes. TU Wien has shown how catalyst design can be advanced for solar-driven NH3 synthesis. Without this chemical technology, feeding the world as we know it would be nearly impossible. The Haber-Bosch process, developed more than a century ago, converts nitrogen from the air into ammonia—the key ingredient in most synthetic fertilizers. Today, roughly half of the world's food production depends on fertilizers derived from ammonia, making the Haber-Bosch process one of the most important industrial innovations in human history.

science.title: Visualizing band structures in nanostructures: Extending band theory to imperfect periodic and bent systems
science.summary: An international collaborative research group has developed a new computational method to visualize the electronic states of aperiodic nanomaterials as band structures through first-principles calculations on finite-sized giant molecule models. The approach reformulates band unfolding for giant molecule models and works even when translational symmetry is imperfect or the material is curved. The team includes Assistant Professor Naoya Yamaguchi and Professor Fumiyuki Ishii of the Nanomaterials Research Institute at Kanazawa University, Associate Professor Chi-Cheng Lee of Tamkang University in Taiwan, and Professor Taisuke Ozaki of the University of Tokyo.

science.title: Coastal land shifts reveal faster local sea level rise than expected
science.summary: For almost a century, researchers have known that vertical land motion—the lifting and sinking of the ground—affects sea level locally. As the ground sinks, the sea level rises relative to the land. Scientists also assumed this process generally occurred at a steady rate over time. But a research team that includes Thomas Wahl, a UCF researcher and associate professor in the Department of Civil, Environmental and Construction Engineering, has found that ground subsidence has undergone phases of variable change, creating significant implications for coastal communities.

science.title: New combined spore trapping and DNA sequencing technology tracks fungicide resistance in grain crops
science.summary: Researchers at the Center for Crop and Disease Management (CCDM) have developed a new system that combines spore trapping with advanced DNA sequencing to confirm fungicide resistance from air samples containing airborne spores.

science.title: Cells have a secret power line: How the nucleus gets its own private energy supply from mitochondria
science.summary: For decades, biologists assumed a cell's energy simply diffused to wherever it was needed. It turns out the most important destination of all has a private delivery line.

science.title: Biopolymer beads extend fungus bioinsecticide shelf life and release
science.summary: Researchers have used a biopolymer to encapsulate and extend the storage life and release rate of a bioinsecticidal fungus. The study is published in ACS Omega. The goal is to extend the shelf life of Beauveria bassiana, a fungus widely used as a bioinsecticide on various agricultural crops. Encapsulation is also a more sustainable alternative because it requires fewer applications and has less potential to affect nontarget species.

science.title: Borneo's ferret badger is found nowhere else on Earth
science.summary: A collaborative study has provided the most comprehensive assessment to date of the endangered Bornean ferret badger (Melogale everetti). Weighing only around one kilogram (2.2 pounds), the Bornean ferret badger is a small, nocturnal carnivore that is rarely seen by people. The paper is published in the journal Ecology and Evolution.

science.title: Drone surveys reveal why steep alpine channels erode so fast during debris flows
science.summary: A brown mass—a mixture of water, boulders and fine matter—plows through the landscape. The mountains wash more than a thousand lorryloads of material into the valley on a fairly regular basis, causing damage in excess of CHF 100 million per year in Switzerland alone. A better understanding of this natural hazard requires data from the debris flow channels, of which there is very little because of the complex surveying process.

science.title: X-rays reveal how platinum oxidizes in real time inside hydrogen devices
science.summary: Electrolysers produce hydrogen. Fuel cells, in turn, generate electricity from hydrogen. Both technologies are considered key building blocks of the energy transition, offering well-established solutions for storing, transporting and producing renewable energy. However, there is a challenge: The platinum catalysts often used in these systems gradually lose performance under high operating loads. In a sense, they "wear out" too quickly, increasing the costs of hydrogen technologies.

science.title: Missing the forest for the trees: Conservationists emphasize the need for intact forest in coffee landscapes
science.summary: As conservationists seek to maintain biodiversity levels in tropical coffee landscapes, data indicate the presence and quality of surrounding forest habitat may play a larger role than previously thought. New research from the Colombian Andes shows that conserving forest cover across coffee-growing landscapes is essential for sustaining diverse bird communities, even more than farm-level shade tree management alone.

science.title: AI helps reveal large-scale quantum effects hidden in stacked atomic sheets
science.summary: Quantum materials are a class of exotic materials with special properties that are governed by quantum mechanics rather than classical physics. Those properties—like superconductivity, entanglement and unusual forms of magnetism—often originate in the tiny repeating patterns of atoms inside crystals, but through clever engineering, they can be observed and controlled at a more human scale. Quantum materials are helping to power the quickly growing field of quantum computing and could find their way into future generations of energy-efficient electronics.

science.title: Farm fields become living labs as data tools reshape crop research
science.summary: For generations, agronomic knowledge has been produced on research stations like AU Flakkebjerg, where fields are designed to answer specific scientific questions. This is where agriculture became measurable, comparable and reproducible. But a quiet shift is underway. Today, experiments are escaping the confines of the research station, turning ordinary farm fields into laboratories and farmers into active contributors to agricultural research.

science.title: Antiviral soil compound disrupts phage infection cycle before viruses can reproduce
science.summary: Bacteria also produce molecules that have an antiviral effect. Researchers from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) and Jülich Research Center (FZJ) have examined the antiviral molecule daunorubicin and decoded its mode of operation against viruses in collaboration with colleagues from Marburg and Zurich. They now describe this mechanism, which primarily targets a specific group of viruses—namely bacteriophages—in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

science.title: Open-source FLIM Playground could speed reproducible analysis of complex cell images
science.summary: Modern fluorescence microscopy can generate images of living cells as stunning to look at as they are informative to study. For techniques like fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM), those images provide a window into cell metabolism, helping scientists study cancer treatment, autoimmune disease and more.

science.title: Algorithm visualizes how cells 'talk' to one another across tissue and time
science.summary: People communicate with each other, sometimes face to face, sometimes with a text message or phone call. Cells also communicate with each other, sometimes by touching and sometimes by sending signals across space and time. But while texts and phone calls can be traced to figure out who is talking to whom, determining which cell is talking to which is exceedingly difficult—until now.

science.title: Gorillas can learn to trust humans even after years of poaching pressure, research shows
science.summary: A new study led by Concordia researchers suggests that carefully habituating wild gorillas to nonthreatening humans can help the animals overcome long-term aversions to people in areas with a history of human-led disturbances such as poaching.

environment.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
environment.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

environment.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
environment.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

environment.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
environment.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

environment.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
environment.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

environment.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
environment.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

environment.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
environment.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

environment.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
environment.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

environment.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

mix_items.title: Stray Lights Festival aduce trei zile de muzică alternativă, post-punk, experimental și noise, între 31 iulie și 2 august, în București, la Control Club
mix_items.summary: Între 31 iulie și 2 august, clubul Control din București devine gazda primei ediții Stray Lights Festival, un nou eveniment dedicat muzicii care există în afara categoriilor și formulelor previzibile.… Articolul Stray Lights Festival aduce trei zile de muzică alternativă, post-punk, experimental și noise, între 31 iulie și 2 august, în București, la Control Club apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Mistreţul văzut ieri în Pipera a fost capturat şi acum e Junior Pig Officer la o multinaţională
mix_items.summary: Niciun animal nu rămâne prea multă vreme în libertate în Pipera! Mistreţul care a stârnit panică şi RO-Alerturi aseară a fost capturat, a trecut interviul şi e încadrat acum ca Junior Pig Officer la o companie multinaţională, anunţă nea Vasile, portarul clădirii de birouri lângă care s-au întâmplat evenimentele. „Fuga, fuga, prin porumb, ştiţi şi […] The post Mistreţul văzut ieri în Pipera a fost capturat şi acum e Junior Pig Officer la o multinaţională appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Înainte de lansarea în România, HONOR Magic V6 a atras deja atenția experților în tehnologie și design, obținând unele dintre cele mai importante distincții acordate în industrie. Într-o categorie în… Articolul HONOR Magic V6 primește premii internaționale înainte de lansarea în România apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Peste 400 de voluntari vor lua cu asalt începând de joi un sat din Timiș pentru a zugrăvi 30 de case în doar trei zile. Satul Macedonia, din județul Timiș,… Articolul Începe Color the Village la Macedonia! apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Pe primul loc în preferințele… Articolul Cele mai apreciate titluri Alice Books la Bookfest 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Autor, actor, motor! Lungmetraje prezentate pe scurt de autori
mix_items.summary: Scurte scene aduse la viață de actori Evenimentul, aflat la a treia ediție, este o prezentare publică a scenariilor de lungmetraj scrise de studenții care au absolvit programele de licență… Articolul Autor, actor, motor! Lungmetraje prezentate pe scurt de autori apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Ciolacu a demisionat din PSD și s-a declarat tehnocrat, iar Tomac l-a numit la Finanțe
mix_items.summary: Achiziție de marcă făcută de guvernul pregătit de Eugen Tomac! Fostul premier Marcel Ciolacu a demisionat din PSD și s-a declarat tehnocrat, așa că Tomac n-a stat pe gânduri și l-a pus deja ministru de Finanțe în guvernul pe care speră să-l conducă în caz că va primi voturile necesare în Parlament. „Domnul Ciolacu este […] The post Ciolacu a demisionat din PSD și s-a declarat tehnocrat, iar Tomac l-a numit la Finanțe appeared first on Times New Roman .

Constanţa Număr de poziții: 2 Contract: full time Grija și încrederea unei familii, emoție pozitivă, zâmbete, suport și încurajare, nevoi de bază îndeplinite și vise transformate... mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... Articolul World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: A hidden DNA genome protector may explain why health and aging differ between men and women
mix_items.summary: How diseases develop and how the body ages can differ between females and males, but the biological reasons for these differences are not fully understood. Researchers are studying the role of sex chromosomes to better understand what may be driving these differences.

mix_items.title: Contabil
mix_items.summary: Locație: București (Satul SOS) Nivel carieră: Mid-level (3-5 ani experiență) Departament: Financiar / Contabilitate Tip job: Full-time Cine suntem noi? La SOS Satele Copiilor România, oferim o familie copiilor ajunși... The post Contabil appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România
mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Degradable sensors reveal hidden soil secrets after microbes nibble on them
mix_items.summary: New degradable sensors could reveal insights into the secret—yet essential—microscopic life in soil. The sensors, which are being developed by soil scientists at Lancaster University and researchers at the University of Colorado Boulder and the University of Manchester, track biological activity in soil by using a degradable substrate that is nibbled on by microbes.

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Lab-created 'moon' rock could help scientists interpret lunar data and explore how water might form on the moon
mix_items.summary: The moon may look unchanged from afar, but its surface is constantly reshaped by microscopic impacts and a steady stream of particles from the sun, a process known as space weathering. Now, Georgia Tech researchers have recreated one of those weathering sources, solar wind, in the lab—offering new insight into how the lunar surface evolves. Their work is published in The Planetary Science Journal.

mix_items.title: Psihopedagog/Pedagog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău Fii adultul care poate schimba viitorul unui copil Dacă ai răbdare, empatie și îți dorești un job cu adevărat valoros, în care fiecare zi contează pentru dezvoltarea unor... The post Psihopedagog/Pedagog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Pregnant women may reduce key health risk through less sitting, more light exercise
mix_items.summary: Women who engage in light physical activity and lessen their sedentary time may significantly reduce the risk of key health problems during pregnancy, according to a new University of Iowa-led study. The paper, "Optimal 24-hour movement behaviour compositions across trimesters and risk of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy: the Pregnancy 24/7 cohort study," is published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine.

mix_items.title: Agent telemarketing (team leader)
mix_items.summary: Locație: București Contract: Part time (remote) Credem în cauza SOS Satele Copiilor și știm că fiecare copil merită să crească într-o familie iubitoare. De 30 de ani SOS Satele Copiilor... The post Agent telemarketing (team leader) appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Low-copper paints matched high-copper rivals, while silicone performed best against fouling
mix_items.summary: When comparing different types of antifouling paints against fouling on leisure boats, the results were the opposite of what many would expect. Of the paints tested, the biocide-free silicone paint worked best, and the paint marketed as environmentally friendly turned out to be extremely toxic. The study, led by Chalmers University of Technology in Sweden, was conducted in Swedish, Danish and French coastal waters and also showed that coatings with a low copper content can be just as effective as those containing more copper.

mix_items.title: Novel catalyst design boosts solar-driven ammonia production under mild conditions
mix_items.summary: Sunlight, water, air and metal-organic catalysts—that could be all it takes. TU Wien has shown how catalyst design can be advanced for solar-driven NH3 synthesis. Without this chemical technology, feeding the world as we know it would be nearly impossible. The Haber-Bosch process, developed more than a century ago, converts nitrogen from the air into ammonia—the key ingredient in most synthetic fertilizers. Today, roughly half of the world's food production depends on fertilizers derived from ammonia, making the Haber-Bosch process one of the most important industrial innovations in human history.

mix_items.title: Transparency is vital for AI usage in health care and patient–provider relationships, researchers find
mix_items.summary: Among the top artificial intelligence companies, the current race is ultimately to build better, faster and more accurate algorithms. Artificial intelligence (AI) has become commonplace in every sector, including health care, and an increasing number of primary care physicians are turning to AI for everything from diagnoses to patient note transcriptions.

mix_items.title: Why the liquid you take with your medication matters
mix_items.summary: Some alkaline mineral and medicinal waters may weaken the enteric coating of medications within just a few minutes, potentially reducing their effectiveness, according to a new study by Semmelweis University. The study, published in Pharmaceutics, found that if the active ingredient is released too early in the stomach rather than in the intestinal tract, it may reduce—and in extreme cases, even eliminate—the effectiveness of certain anti-reflux, gastroprotective, psychiatric or anti-inflammatory pain-relief medications.

mix_items.title: Visualizing band structures in nanostructures: Extending band theory to imperfect periodic and bent systems
mix_items.summary: An international collaborative research group has developed a new computational method to visualize the electronic states of aperiodic nanomaterials as band structures through first-principles calculations on finite-sized giant molecule models. The approach reformulates band unfolding for giant molecule models and works even when translational symmetry is imperfect or the material is curved. The team includes Assistant Professor Naoya Yamaguchi and Professor Fumiyuki Ishii of the Nanomaterials Research Institute at Kanazawa University, Associate Professor Chi-Cheng Lee of Tamkang University in Taiwan, and Professor Taisuke Ozaki of the University of Tokyo.

mix_items.title: From skipping meals to selling assets: COVID-19 and coping strategies of vulnerable Indian households
mix_items.summary: The COVID-19 pandemic pushed some households in India into difficult and often unsustainable coping strategies, forcing tradeoffs between immediate survival and long-term stability, according to new research by Lancaster University and the Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur (IITK).

mix_items.title: Coastal land shifts reveal faster local sea level rise than expected
mix_items.summary: For almost a century, researchers have known that vertical land motion—the lifting and sinking of the ground—affects sea level locally. As the ground sinks, the sea level rises relative to the land. Scientists also assumed this process generally occurred at a steady rate over time. But a research team that includes Thomas Wahl, a UCF researcher and associate professor in the Department of Civil, Environmental and Construction Engineering, has found that ground subsidence has undergone phases of variable change, creating significant implications for coastal communities.

mix_items.title: New combined spore trapping and DNA sequencing technology tracks fungicide resistance in grain crops
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Center for Crop and Disease Management (CCDM) have developed a new system that combines spore trapping with advanced DNA sequencing to confirm fungicide resistance from air samples containing airborne spores.

mix_items.title: Girls are biggest consumers of anxiolytic and sedative drugs among adolescents in most EU countries
mix_items.summary: Xabi Martinez-Mendia, a researcher at the EHU-University of the Basque Country, has analyzed how adolescent psychotropic drug consumption is linked to gender inequality and economic factors in 32 European countries. Published in the European Journal of Public Health, the results indicate that overall consumption of psychotropic drugs is on the rise in countries with greater gender and economic parity, whereas in countries with lower parity, these drugs are consumed proportionally more by girls, and the gender gap is wider.

mix_items.title: Cells have a secret power line: How the nucleus gets its own private energy supply from mitochondria
mix_items.summary: For decades, biologists assumed a cell's energy simply diffused to wherever it was needed. It turns out the most important destination of all has a private delivery line.

mix_items.title: Biopolymer beads extend fungus bioinsecticide shelf life and release
mix_items.summary: Researchers have used a biopolymer to encapsulate and extend the storage life and release rate of a bioinsecticidal fungus. The study is published in ACS Omega. The goal is to extend the shelf life of Beauveria bassiana, a fungus widely used as a bioinsecticide on various agricultural crops. Encapsulation is also a more sustainable alternative because it requires fewer applications and has less potential to affect nontarget species.

mix_items.title: Borneo's ferret badger is found nowhere else on Earth
mix_items.summary: A collaborative study has provided the most comprehensive assessment to date of the endangered Bornean ferret badger (Melogale everetti). Weighing only around one kilogram (2.2 pounds), the Bornean ferret badger is a small, nocturnal carnivore that is rarely seen by people. The paper is published in the journal Ecology and Evolution.

mix_items.title: Drug reduces the risk of developing rheumatoid arthritis, clinical trial finds
mix_items.summary: A clinical trial led by researchers from Hospital Clínic de Barcelona, the August Pi i Sunyer Biomedical Research Institute (IDIBAPS) and the Institute of Advanced Chemistry of Catalonia (IQAC-CSIC) compared the effectiveness of abatacept and hydroxychloroquine in preventing the development of rheumatoid arthritis in patients with palindromic rheumatism, an autoimmune disease that progresses to arthritis in approximately half of all patients.

mix_items.title: Drone surveys reveal why steep alpine channels erode so fast during debris flows
mix_items.summary: A brown mass—a mixture of water, boulders and fine matter—plows through the landscape. The mountains wash more than a thousand lorryloads of material into the valley on a fairly regular basis, causing damage in excess of CHF 100 million per year in Switzerland alone. A better understanding of this natural hazard requires data from the debris flow channels, of which there is very little because of the complex surveying process.

mix_items.title: Online grocery shopping could bring more fresh produce to New York's SNAP families
mix_items.summary: The high cost of fresh fruits and vegetables and the need to restock them frequently can be barriers to healthy eating for low-income families. New research shows that online grocery shopping, combined with incentive programs, can help.

mix_items.title: X-rays reveal how platinum oxidizes in real time inside hydrogen devices
mix_items.summary: Electrolysers produce hydrogen. Fuel cells, in turn, generate electricity from hydrogen. Both technologies are considered key building blocks of the energy transition, offering well-established solutions for storing, transporting and producing renewable energy. However, there is a challenge: The platinum catalysts often used in these systems gradually lose performance under high operating loads. In a sense, they "wear out" too quickly, increasing the costs of hydrogen technologies.

mix_items.title: How modern rheumatoid arthritis treatments can protect bone health
mix_items.summary: A new review published in Calcified Tissue International highlights major advances in understanding and preventing bone loss in people with rheumatoid arthritis (RA), showing that modern antirheumatic therapies can significantly reduce local and generalized bone loss in RA.

mix_items.title: Missing the forest for the trees: Conservationists emphasize the need for intact forest in coffee landscapes
mix_items.summary: As conservationists seek to maintain biodiversity levels in tropical coffee landscapes, data indicate the presence and quality of surrounding forest habitat may play a larger role than previously thought. New research from the Colombian Andes shows that conserving forest cover across coffee-growing landscapes is essential for sustaining diverse bird communities, even more than farm-level shade tree management alone.

mix_items.title: Surgical menopause causes more severe genitourinary syndrome of menopause phenotype
mix_items.summary: Menopause is associated with an array of sexual and urinary symptoms, although not all women experience the same symptoms or severity. A new study suggests that many of these signs and symptoms are worse for women who undergo surgical menopause instead of natural menopause. Results of the study are published today in Menopause.

mix_items.title: AI helps reveal large-scale quantum effects hidden in stacked atomic sheets
mix_items.summary: Quantum materials are a class of exotic materials with special properties that are governed by quantum mechanics rather than classical physics. Those properties—like superconductivity, entanglement and unusual forms of magnetism—often originate in the tiny repeating patterns of atoms inside crystals, but through clever engineering, they can be observed and controlled at a more human scale. Quantum materials are helping to power the quickly growing field of quantum computing and could find their way into future generations of energy-efficient electronics.

mix_items.title: Farm fields become living labs as data tools reshape crop research
mix_items.summary: For generations, agronomic knowledge has been produced on research stations like AU Flakkebjerg, where fields are designed to answer specific scientific questions. This is where agriculture became measurable, comparable and reproducible. But a quiet shift is underway. Today, experiments are escaping the confines of the research station, turning ordinary farm fields into laboratories and farmers into active contributors to agricultural research.

mix_items.title: Decades-old puzzle solved as scientists uncover cause of inflammatory bowel disease
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Nuffield Department of Medicine, University of Oxford, together with Newcastle University's Translational and Clinical Research Institute and the Department of Immunology at Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, have identified an important driver of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). This discovery reshapes understanding of IBD and opens the way to targeted approaches to diagnosis and treatment in a subset of patients. The findings suggest that inflammatory bowel disease is not a single condition, but a group of biologically distinct diseases driven by different underlying mechanisms.

mix_items.title: Antiviral soil compound disrupts phage infection cycle before viruses can reproduce
mix_items.summary: Bacteria also produce molecules that have an antiviral effect. Researchers from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) and Jülich Research Center (FZJ) have examined the antiviral molecule daunorubicin and decoded its mode of operation against viruses in collaboration with colleagues from Marburg and Zurich. They now describe this mechanism, which primarily targets a specific group of viruses—namely bacteriophages—in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

mix_items.title: New protein targets reveal 13 drug repurposing leads for progressive multiple sclerosis
mix_items.summary: A new study identified protein targets and drug candidates, including opportunities to repurpose existing drugs. Six key proteins were highlighted as potentially providing new insights and supporting future treatment development for progressive multiple sclerosis (MS).

mix_items.title: Difficulty conceiving tied to small neurodevelopmental differences in children
mix_items.summary: Difficulty conceiving a pregnancy may be associated with small differences in children's learning and behavior, according to a new study, published in the journal JAMA Network Open, titled "Associations of subfecundity and infertility treatment with child neurodevelopment in ECHO."

mix_items.title: Open-source FLIM Playground could speed reproducible analysis of complex cell images
mix_items.summary: Modern fluorescence microscopy can generate images of living cells as stunning to look at as they are informative to study. For techniques like fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM), those images provide a window into cell metabolism, helping scientists study cancer treatment, autoimmune disease and more.

mix_items.title: Ten-week therapy empowers parents to solve severe selective eating in children with autism
mix_items.summary: Picky eating is a challenge most parents are familiar with, but for parents of autistic children, severe selective eating can lead to nutritional deficiencies and place tremendous stress on the family. However, a new study from Constructor University Ph.D. candidate Sofya Bajaa has demonstrated a transformative new approach to treating severe selective eating in children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Bajaa's newly developed Schmetterling Nutritional Behavior Intervention (NBI) program achieved dramatic improvements in dietary variety and nutritional intake over just 10 weeks.

mix_items.title: Algorithm visualizes how cells 'talk' to one another across tissue and time
mix_items.summary: People communicate with each other, sometimes face to face, sometimes with a text message or phone call. Cells also communicate with each other, sometimes by touching and sometimes by sending signals across space and time. But while texts and phone calls can be traced to figure out who is talking to whom, determining which cell is talking to which is exceedingly difficult—until now.

mix_items.title: Gorillas can learn to trust humans even after years of poaching pressure, research shows
mix_items.summary: A new study led by Concordia researchers suggests that carefully habituating wild gorillas to nonthreatening humans can help the animals overcome long-term aversions to people in areas with a history of human-led disturbances such as poaching.

mix_items.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
mix_items.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

mix_items.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
mix_items.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

mix_items.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
mix_items.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

mix_items.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
mix_items.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

mix_items.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
mix_items.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

mix_items.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
mix_items.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

mix_items.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
mix_items.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

joke_ro.text: Nu sunt lenes. Sunt pe modul economie de energie.

satire_ro.title: Nu doar urşii sunt periculoşi! Turist ardelean, atacat fulgerător de o ţestoasă în Munţii Dobrogei
